addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

7	// You stand at the ship's command pass. Six subagents rebuild the ship in
8	// parallel; each finished job rides up the pass as silent frozen work:
9	//
10	//   tap silent frozen work → `entire checkpoint explain <id>` runs, the card opens
11	//   read what it did       → drag it to the lit ship slot, online

14	// The LAUNCH WINDOW (clock) runs the whole time, so clearing the pass with
15	// `explain` IS the rush. Records never expire — the pressure is the flood: the
16	// bigger your unreviewed pile, the FASTER the window drains (the ship can't
17	// stabilise on work nobody has accounted for).

19	// ONE visible pressure: the launch clock. It always ticks at 1×, and two
20	// untreated things speed it up: hot red pips waiting on the dispatch board, and
21	// sealed work piling on the belt. Dispatching a hot pip relieves board pressure;
22	// running explain on a block relieves review pressure.

340	function makeLabelSprite(text, { color = "#dff1ff", weight = 700, px = 52 } = {}) {
341	  const pad = 24;
342	  const c = document.createElement("canvas");
343	  const ctx = c.getContext("2d");
344	  ctx.font = `${weight} ${px}px "Segoe UI", system-ui, sans-serif`;
345	  const w = Math.ceil(ctx.measureText(text).width) + pad * 2;
346	  const h = px + pad * 2;

463	// The ship hangs above a conveyor. Each part has a labeled slot on the ship.

content.text:
Read the whole thing. Here's my honest take on Level 2 as gameplay — not just whether it's clever, but whether it's *fun*.

## The core loop is genuinely good
The two-front pressure is the strongest thing here, and it's fully wired (I checked — `update()` at line 1078 drains the clock by *both* `SEAL_DRAIN * unreviewedCount()` and `DOT_DRAIN * activeDotHeat()`, and dots heat up the longer you ignore them at line 1076):

- **Dispatch dots fast** before they heat up and start burning the window, but…
- **…every dispatch makes a sealed block** that *also* burns the window until you `explain` it.

That's a real throughput dilemma — you can't just spam all 6 dots at t=0, because then the belt floods and the pile drains you faster than you can click through it. Managing the *rate* is the game. That's legit Overcooked DNA, and it maps beautifully onto the actual lesson: parallel subagents produce work faster than you can review it, and unreviewed work is a liability. The metaphor and the mechanic are saying the same thing, which is rare and good.

The payoff line — *"look how much got done without you"* — lands the theme perfectly.

## What I think undercuts the fun

**1. The drag-to-match is fake tension.** This is my biggest concern. After `explain`, the block gets tagged with `🔧 Engine` (line ~883), and the ship slot is labeled `🔧 Engine` (line 560). So "drag it to the bay you *think* it matches" and "you'll find out when you dispatch" is theater — it's a 1:1 icon+label lookup. You basically can't get it wrong unless you misclick. The deferred grading (`gradeAndDispatch`, blocks ride back on the belt) adds ceremony and a punishing-feeling bounce-back for what is, in practice, a non-decision. Either make the match require *reading the explain card* (e.g., the block shows the *symptom* or the *became* name, and you infer the system), or drop the suspense framing and let it snap in on drop. Right now it's busywork dressed as a puzzle.

**2. It's probably too easy / too long once you "get it."** 90s for 6 parts where the hardest action is clicking. The panic sky and clock *feel* tense, but a player who understands the loop (dispatch a few, explain as they land, keep the belt short) will finish with ~40s to spare. First playthrough = exciting chaos; second = solved. The shuffle (`slotLanes`, `pendingQueue`) only defeats positional muscle memory — since matching is by label, it doesn't add difficulty. Consider whether L2 should ramp (more parts, or a tighter window) or stay deliberately gentle as a teaching beat.

**3. A lot of vertical mouse travel.** Dots live high on the board (`DOT_Y 4.6` → screen-high), slates ride low on the belt, and you drag *back up* to slots at `SHIP_Y 9.5`. Three zones, constant up-down-up. In a timed rush with a perspective camera and a fixed `DRAG_DEPTH` of 22, dropping onto high slots can get fiddly. It's the kind of thing that reads fine in code and feels twitchy in hand — worth watching in playtest.

**4. Two input grammars at once.** Click-dot-to-dispatch + click-slate-to-explain + drag-slate-to-install is a fair amount to hold. It's *learnable* (the two briefing beats + `teachOnce` prompts do a lot of work), but it's denser than Level 1's single shoot-to-bank verb. That's the cost of teaching two commands in one level.

## Verdict
The *systems design* is the best in the game so far — the dual drain is a real, theme-aligned decision space. Whether it's **fun** hinges almost entirely on fixing #1: right now the climax of every round (the matching) is the one part with no actual decision in it, so the tension you built up with the clock has nowhere to discharge. Make the match require a beat of thought from the explain card and this goes from "clever and a bit hollow" to "actually satisfying."

content.text:
Done. Here's what changed in `src/droneBayView.js`:

**`placePart` now grades on drop** — if you drag a block into a bay that isn't its match, it bounces straight back to the belt, throws a red spark on the wrong bay, and tells you exactly what went wrong:

> ✗ wrong bay — 🔧 Engine doesn't belong in 📡 Radio

First time it happens you also get a one-shot coaching line ("match the block's label to the bay's label… try the right square"). Only the correct bay accepts the block.

**Knock-on cleanups:**
- `gradeAndDispatch` is simplified — since a wrong placement can never stick now, `entire dispatch` just locks the matched blocks in green and files the report (no more deferred reveal / bounce-back-at-the-end).
- Updated the "all placed" prompt and a few comments that still promised end-of-round flagging.

Syntax checks clean.

One honest note on feel: the bounce makes mistakes *instantly legible*, which is the right call — but because the block tag and the bay label are still the identical `icon + name`, it's hard to actually land in the wrong bay unless you misclick. So this mostly turns the old fake-suspense into honest, snappy feedback rather than adding a real puzzle. If you want the *matching itself* to require a beat of thought, the next step would be to show something other than the literal name on the block (e.g. the symptom — "engine seized" — or the `became` name "Plasma Ring") so the player has to connect it to the 🔧 Engine bay. Want me to try that?

message.content: i have a question..the dots show urgency when you take too long to dispatch..should we do the same for the squares that you have to match

content.text:
Good instinct, but I'd push back on putting it on the *squares* specifically. Here's how I read the pressure model as it stands:

You currently have two escalating pressures, and both sit on **work you've surfaced but haven't acted on**:

- **Undispatched dot** → heats up (`DOT_HEAT_RATE`), drains via `DOT_DRAIN * activeDotHeat()`. Relieved by dispatching.
- **Unreviewed sealed block** → drains via `SEAL_DRAIN * unreviewedCount()`. Relieved by `explain`.

The square is different in kind — it's a *destination*, not a backlog. An empty bay isn't "work piling up that you're ignoring"; there may not even be a block for it yet. So a pulsing "hurry up!" on an empty square is urgency the player can't act on — urgent *for what?* That tends to read as noise, not pressure.

There's also a specific conflict: making the *matching* bay light up would undo a deliberate choice in your code — `// No drag highlight — dropping is blind; you match by reading the labels.` Urgency on the target square is half a step from "here's where it goes," which kills the read-the-label moment you intentionally built.

**Where the urgency actually belongs, if you want a third beat:** the *reviewed-but-not-yet-installed block*. Right now there's a dead spot — the moment you `explain` a block, `unreviewedCount()` drops it and all its drain pressure releases. So you can explain all six (kill all seal pressure), then install at your leisure with zero clock cost. That's the one gap in the otherwise-tight loop. Closing it would mean: an explained block left sitting on the belt slowly "goes stale" and resumes a small drain — mirroring the dot's heat language so it reads consistently (block rim shifts amber→red as it waits).

That said — my honest recommendation is **probably don't add it yet.** Two fronts (dispatch rate vs. review rate) is already a lot to juggle, and because the match itself is trivial (label = label), install-speed pressure tests mouse dexterity, not decisions — which is the least satisfying thing to punish. I'd only add the stale-block drain if playtesting shows people exploiting the "explain everything, then relax" gap.

So, three options as I see them:

1. **Leave squares alone, do nothing** (recommended) — the two-front model is clean; don't risk tipping it into unfair.
2. **Stale reviewed-block drain** — close the explain-then-dawdle gap, urgency on the block (not the square), consistent with dot heat.
3. **Urgency on the squares** — I'd advise against; it's pressure you can't act on and it leaks the answer.

Which way do you want to go?

content.text:
No — different part. Let me lay out the anatomy, because the slate has a few pieces (from `buildSlate`, line ~383):

A **slate** (the work tile riding the belt) is made of:
- **slab** — the dark base plate
- **rim** — the glowing edge around it (`g.userData.rim`)
- **ice** — the frozen shell on top (`makeIceBlock()`) = the "sealed, not yet explained" signal
- **upgradeModel** — the actual improvised thing (plasma ring, star dome…), hidden until you explain

The lifecycle of those visuals:
- **On the belt, sealed:** ice ON, upgrade hidden, rim dim cyan (`0x6fe3ff`, opacity 0.5)
- **After `explain`:** upgrade becomes visible, name tag appears, rim brightens (`0x8fe3ff`, 0.85) — line 1131–1132
- **On install into a bay:** ice finally turns off (line 922)

So when I said the block "goes stale, rim shifts amber→red," I meant the **rim** — the glowing edge — not the ice. The ice is the *sealed* indicator; by the time a block is in the "reviewed-but-not-installed" stale window, it's already been explained, so the ice no longer represents its state.

(Side note I noticed while checking: the ice stays visible from explain all the way until install — so a reviewed block still looks frozen even though you've already opened it. That's a small visual inconsistency regardless of the staleness question — arguably the ice should crack/vanish *on explain*, not on install, since explain is the "unsealing" moment.)

So: the rim is the natural place to show staleness. Does that match what you were picturing, or were you thinking the ice should be the thing that reacts?

**New third front: reviewed work goes stale.** Once you `explain` a block, it's unsealed (the ice now cracks off *on explain*, not on install — fixing that inconsistency we spotted). It then sits on the belt with a grace period, after which it starts to "go stale":
- Its **rim ages cyan → amber → red** (`staleHeatOf`), pulsing faster as it gets critical.
- A stale block **resumes draining the clock** (`STALE_DRAIN * totalStaleHeat()`), closing the old "explain everything, then relax" gap.
- It feeds the **panic sky** gently, and the HUD prompt switches to *"Reviewed work is going stale — install it before the rim turns red."*

Crucially, this lives on the **block**, not the squares — so it doesn't leak which bay is the answer (your deliberate "match by reading labels" choice stays intact).

**Tunables (top of file), so you can dial it after a playtest:**
- `STALE_GRACE = 6.0` — seconds of free time before a reviewed block starts aging
- `STALE_RATE = 0.07` — ~14s from grace-end to fully stale
- `STALE_DRAIN = 0.10` — max extra drain per fully-stale block (gentler than the `0.18` unreviewed-seal drain — you've already done the review)

I kept it intentionally mild so it just discourages hoarding rather than turning into a frantic third clock. My honest expectation: with the match being trivial, this mostly punishes *hoarding*, not decisions — which is the right thing to punish here, since the original gap was "explain all 6, then dawdle."

content.text:
Yes — exactly. These blocks *are* the customers. In Diner Dash / Overcooked a waiting customer (or order ticket) has a **patience meter**: it sits there ticking down, and if you don't serve them in time they leave angry — you lose that order and have to eat the cost.

And sorry — "maxes out the drain" was bad jargon on my part. Ignore it. What I'd built was abstract and unreadable: blocks you ignored were silently making the launch clock tick *faster* in the background, with no clear "this specific block is the problem." Your instinct is the better one — make it concrete and physical like Overcooked, not an invisible math penalty.

So here's the clean version, in Diner Dash terms:

- **The ice is the patience meter.** Fresh block = solid ice. The longer it waits, the more it reddens and starts to melt.
- **If it fully melts, the order is lost** — the work spoils, the block leaves the belt, and that part's **red dot comes back on the dispatch board**. You have to dispatch a subagent, wait, explain, and match it all over again. The punishment is the *time you lose*, racing the launch clock — same as a customer storming out costs you in Diner Dash.
- No more invisible drain. One visible meter per block. You can see exactly which block is about to die.

The one thing I still need from you — **when does the patience start ticking?**

- **From the moment it lands on the belt** — you're racing to explain *and* match it before it melts. (This is what Overcooked does: the ticket timer starts the second the order appears.)
- **Only after you explain it** — the meter is just on the *match* step; an un-explained sealed block waits forever until you open it.

I lean toward "from arrival" — it's truer to the genre and keeps the belt from becoming a safe parking lot.

And one practical bit, since you chose **fully sealed** (no peeking at what it is): with several identical ice blocks waiting, you'll need *some* way to tell them apart without giving away the answer. I'd put the neutral **checkpoint hash** on the ice (`a1c9e4…`) — it labels the block so you can track it and re-click to re-read its explain card, but it tells you nothing about which bay it goes in. Sound right?

4	// LEVEL 2 ("The Drone Bay") — a COMMAND PASS / order-ticket rush.
5	// (Full design notes live above createDroneBayView, further down this file.)
6	//
7	// You stand at the ship's command pass. Six subagents rebuild the ship in
8	// parallel; each finished job rides up the pass as silent frozen work:
9	//
10	//   tap silent frozen work → `entire checkpoint explain <id>` runs, the card opens
11	//   read what it did       → drag it to the matching ship slot
12	//   all parts online      → type `entire dispatch` — the day's report writes itself
13	//
14	// The LAUNCH WINDOW (clock) runs the whole time, so clearing the pass with
15	// `explain` IS the rush. Records never expire — the pressure is the flood: the
16	// bigger your unreviewed pile, the FASTER the window drains (the ship can't
17	// stabilise on work nobody has accounted for).
18	
19	// ONE visible pressure: the launch clock. It always ticks at 1×, and two
20	// untreated things speed it up: aged red pips waiting on the dispatch board,
21	// and sealed work piling on the belt. Dispatching an aged pip relieves board
22	// pressure; running explain on a block relieves review pressure.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: level2-drone-bay-design
3	description: "Agreed L2 (2026-06-10) — 'The Drone Bay' teaches `entire checkpoint explain`; Archive/search level shelved for later"
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: d43002f0-2256-4a4e-8abe-ae97243b5906
8	---
9	
10	Agreed with Rizel 2026-06-10, replacing the built Archive level (shelved, kept in `src/archiveView.js`, playable at `?view=archive`). BUILT 2026-06-10 in `src/droneBayView.js` (#db-hud markup, post-L1 pin + ?view=level2 route here now); 150s clock, 18s drone work time, both tunable. Drones IMPROVISE (Rizel's "bow turns into a sword"): coils→Plasma Ring, nav core→Star Dome, dish→Signal Spire, scrubbers→Garden Pod, struts→Grav Skid — the transformation creates the "what did you DO?" urge that explain satisfies. Beam-color status grammar: amber broken → cyan working → ice-blue review-me → none (upgrade is the landmark). Ground levels in the day-to-day agentic workflow: **delegate → review → accept**. Level 2 "The Drone Bay": 5 broken ship systems (ignition coils, nav core, antenna, life support, landing struts), 5 drones (one per job, no economy). Assign with E (drone flies out and works); player chooses serial vs. parallel — the one countdown makes parallel the rational move. Finished sites seal under a checkpoint; walk up → terminal opens **pre-filled** with `entire checkpoint explain <id>` (Rizel: never type ids) → Enter → story card of what the drone did → **ADD TO SHIP** confirm (Y) → meter +20%. Finish line (changed 2026-06-10): the FIFTH accept keeps the terminal open and you type `entire dispatch` right there — the day's report writes itself from the checkpoints ("Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work" per the real CLI). Rizel couldn't find the end-of-level console ("where am I supposed to be going") → no final fetch-quest; next command at the prompt you're already at. `entire checkpoint list` still accepted as an optional L1 callback. Bird's-eye map (M, src/overhead.js) added to all island levels; terminals stay open over it. Deliberately excluded: botched-drone deduction puzzles, drone management, extracting values from explanations (Rizel rejected those as "too complicated"). Teaching — use Rizel's framing (2026-06-10), not workflow jargon like "delegate→review→accept": the level shows **multiple agents working in parallel, seeing what work each did (explain), and getting a summary at the end (dispatch)**. Sending a drone is one button (spectacle, not a lesson); ADD TO SHIP is just the gate that makes reading the explanation non-skippable. Terminology (Rizel, 2026-06-10): in all player-facing copy the workers are called **subagents**, never drones — only the location keeps the name "The Drone Bay" (possible later rename; candidates discussed: The Fleet, Mission Control, fiction-patch "drones piloted by subagents"). Why: the word should make the real-world mapping unmistakable — these are agents working in parallel (or sequentially, player's choice) and you get insight into their work. Not metaphor decoration. See [[level2-archive-search-design]] and [[level2-design-direction]].
11	
12	**REWORKED 2026-06-16 — starting-grid / mission-control rebuild.** Rizel disliked walking the island to scattered sites; redesigned the dispatch layer (NOT the explain/dispatch teaching, which is unchanged) into a fixed-camera racing-grid. droneBayView.js rewritten: dropped first-person (firstPerson.js), overhead map (overhead.js), pointer-lock, walk-up proximity, and the E-assign key. Now: fixed camera over 5 parallel lanes; subagents are **cars** (not drones) parked on a grid, rails drawn along terrain. **Click a car = arm; Space = green flag (launches all armed at once); click a moving car = nitro boost.** This one mechanic gives both sequential (arm-one-then-Space) and parallel (arm all five) — Rizel explicitly wanted both. Nitro = the engagement-during-travel + catch-up tool (3 charges/run, shown as dots in #db-systems-rows); Rizel didn't care what "powerups" meant, "just something where they go faster." Lanes are LOCAL to L2 (props placed at lane-ends, START_Z=64→FINISH_Z=-34, slight fan) and decoupled from SYSTEMS.pos — left SYSTEMS.pos untouched because launchView.js (L3) still reads it for its own island. Tunables: BASE_SPEED 1/14, BOOST_MULT 2.6, BOOST_DUR 2.2, NITRO_MAX 3, TOTAL_TIME 150. Kept: clock+panic sky, checkpoint cards, click-sealed-car→pre-filled explain→ADD TO SHIP→dispatch finish, win/fail. Open/untested live: car speed feel, whether any lane-end finish lands in water. Briefing + CONTROLS in index.html (#db-briefing) rewritten to match (click/Space, no WASD/E/M).
13	
14	**TENSION REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch grid (drag + generic hazards).** Playtest of the starting-grid version: "kinda good but no tension." Diagnosed: cars were already on rails (just flipping a switch), clock far too generous (150s vs ~14s parallel), nitro had no job, parallel overlap means the slowest lane sets finish time so an aggregate clock CAN'T create parallel tension (mathematically — boosting only the slowest barely helps). Conclusion: parallel "chaos" tension MUST come from a per-lane failure stake, not the clock. Agreed direction with Rizel: **true drag + generic hazards** (rejected specialty-matching cars/Decoder/Repair — it's the "drone management" she already killed, blows the 15s onboarding, and telegraphs outcomes which guts the explain payoff). Built in droneBayView.js: cars now wait in a BAY (BAY_Z=START_Z+26=90), tracks start EMPTY. Verbs: **drag** a car bay→track (loads at line), **Space** green-flags all loaded, **click** a bogged running car to nitro through. Decoupled car pool (`cars[]`, state bay→loaded→running→done) from `lanes[]` (state empty→loaded→running→sealed→added); lane.carObj links them. Drag = mousedown/mousemove(>5px)/mouseup, ground-plane raycast for position, nearestEmptyLane (|Δx|<9 & z<BAY_Z-10) for drop; click vs drag disambiguated by movement. Each lane has a corruption patch (HAZARDS[]: 2 severe + 3 mild, staggered): inside, speed×HAZARD_SLOW(0.30); SEVERE lanes also fill `corrupt` over HAZARD_FILL(3.6s) → STALL (crawl at STALL_CRAWL 0.06, basically stuck) unless boosted; boost (nitro--) clears the patch + un-stalls. NITRO_MAX=3 < 5 cars = the triage choice (cover the 2 severe). **Clock is now the RESCUE RUN**: starts on first green flag (clockStarted), stops the moment allHome() (every lane sealed/added) — so reviewing explanations is calm/untimed (protects the lesson). TOTAL_TIME=50, BASE_SPEED=1/12, LOW_TIME=18/CRIT=8/PANIC=22 — all tunable, NOT yet playtested. Fail modes that create tension: sequential bleeds the clock, a car left in the bay never homes (clock kills you), wasting nitro on mild lanes → severe stalls → fail. Hazard discs (buildHazard) sit on tracks so you can read severity before launch; drop-target lane rails brighten cyan while dragging; is-stalled CSS row = red blink "BOGGED". Briefing/controls rewritten again (drag/Space/click). Rizel will feel-out & tune the constants (she did this for L1 timer).

She chose tactile per-subagent sending for the delegate feel + a parallel-vs-sequential stagger choice. **RE-SEND 2026-06-16** (Rizel: "we only sent out 6 subagents, so how did we get all 6/12 fixed records" — the silent auto-2nd-job broke the count's logic): each drone now does ONE job per dispatch, flies home and goes idle (SEND cue returns), and YOU re-dispatch it for its 2nd job. 6 drones × you sending each twice = 12 records — the count is a direct result of player action, and "send subagents" stays a live verb the whole run (you re-send while reviewing; an idle drone with a job left = work not happening = the clock keeps bleeding). sendable(w)=phase==="idle"&&queue.length; "done"=no jobs left, parked dim. **DRONE LOOK + VISIBLE FLY-OUT 2026-06-16** (Rizel: subagents "should look like something — drones/robots," and she wants to SEE them "fly off and work on repairing the things before it piles up a backlog"): buildWorker→**buildDrone**. (First pass was a quadcopter w/ rotors+eye+welder; Rizel preferred an EARLIER drone look from a past version. **Found via the entire-search agent in commit 0553cd7** "Add Level 2 'The Drone Bay'" — a dark metallic OctahedronGeometry(0.9) body, emissive 0x2a8aa6, wrapped in a spinning cyan TorusGeometry halo (orig 1.0r, now 1.25r); halo spin speeds up idle→flight→work like the original. RESTORED that.) It hovers on a landing pad with the SEND ▸ ring/label + a hover underglow; welds with amber sparks under the body at the hull. On dispatch the drone runs a phase machine (idle→out→work→back→done): lifts off and FLIES to a hull work-site (up at SITE_Y 8, back toward the ship board at SITE_Z -16) over FLY_DUR 1.1s, WELDS in place (eye brightens, amber sparks at the tool tip) for WORK_TIME, emits the finished slate to the pass, then flies home (re-send for its 2nd job — see RE-SEND above; parks dim once its queue is empty). So you watch the crew work the hull before any backlog builds. Verified headlessly (CDP): fly-out, weld-sparks, flood, explain, add — zero exceptions. Round-robin 2 jobs each (WORK_TIME 6.5 ±2.5 jitter) → finished job pushed to `pass[]`, slate slides in from ENTRANCE_X to its slot (VISIBLE_SLOTS=5, SLOT_X0 -16 / SLOT_DX 8; extras back up off-screen but still count). **Click slate = run explain** (focuses + flips, card in repurposed #terminal as a NON-blocking panel — pointer stays live so the pass keeps flooding while you read); **Y or ADD TO SHIP button** = added → slate flies to its hull pip, ice melts, pip greens. All 12 added → clock stops, terminal switches to blocking "report" mode → type `entire dispatch` → win. Fail = clock hits 0 (drain = dt·(1 + RAIL_DRAIN·pass.length), RAIL_DRAIN 0.14). Tunables (UNPLAYTESTED, Rizel will feel out like the L1 timer): TOTAL_TIME 75, WORK_TIME 6.5, WORK_JITTER 2.5, RAIL_DRAIN 0.14, VISIBLE_SLOTS 5, BACKLOG_PANIC 5, N_WORKERS 6. L3 untouched — SYSTEMS (with .pos) + UPGRADE_BUILDERS still exported and consumed by launchView.js. index.html: db-fail copy updated (no more "use the fleet"); db-briefing already the 2-beat mission-banner; all db-/term-/cta- CSS classes reused. **VERIFIED via headless-Chrome CDP drive (node /tmp/cdp.mjs): full loop runs with ZERO exceptions — briefing→flood→click-explain→Y-add→pip green→pass reflow.** Screenshots confirm layout reads well; slates are large/slightly cramped at SLOT_DX 8 (candidate tune). Dev server: `npm run dev` (was on :5174). OPEN/likely-to-tune: clock vs work-speed feel, slate spacing/scale, whether the non-blocking panel wants a visible "focused slate" tie-line.
40

file.content:
---
name: level2-drone-bay-design
description: "Agreed L2 (2026-06-10) — 'The Drone Bay' teaches `entire checkpoint explain`; Archive/search level shelved for later"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: d43002f0-2256-4a4e-8abe-ae97243b5906
---

Agreed with Rizel 2026-06-10, replacing the built Archive level (shelved, kept in `src/archiveView.js`, playable at `?view=archive`). BUILT 2026-06-10 in `src/droneBayView.js` (#db-hud markup, post-L1 pin + ?view=level2 route here now); 150s clock, 18s drone work time, both tunable. Drones IMPROVISE (Rizel's "bow turns into a sword"): coils→Plasma Ring, nav core→Star Dome, dish→Signal Spire, scrubbers→Garden Pod, struts→Grav Skid — the transformation creates the "what did you DO?" urge that explain satisfies. Beam-color status grammar: amber broken → cyan working → ice-blue review-me → none (upgrade is the landmark). Ground levels in the day-to-day agentic workflow: **delegate → review → accept**. Level 2 "The Drone Bay": 5 broken ship systems (ignition coils, nav core, antenna, life support, landing struts), 5 drones (one per job, no economy). Assign with E (drone flies out and works); player chooses serial vs. parallel — the one countdown makes parallel the rational move. Finished sites seal under a checkpoint; walk up → terminal opens **pre-filled** with `entire checkpoint explain <id>` (Rizel: never type ids) → Enter → story card of what the drone did → **ADD TO SHIP** confirm (Y) → meter +20%. Finish line (changed 2026-06-10): the FIFTH accept keeps the terminal open and you type `entire dispatch` right there — the day's report writes itself from the checkpoints ("Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work" per the real CLI). Rizel couldn't find the end-of-level console ("where am I supposed to be going") → no final fetch-quest; next command at the prompt you're already at. `entire checkpoint list` still accepted as an optional L1 callback. Bird's-eye map (M, src/overhead.js) added to all island levels; terminals stay open over it. Deliberately excluded: botched-drone deduction puzzles, drone management, extracting values from explanations (Rizel rejected those as "too complicated"). Teaching — use Rizel's framing (2026-06-10), not workflow jargon like "delegate→review→accept": the level shows **multiple agents working in parallel, seeing what work each did (explain), and getting a summary at the end (dispatch)**. Sending a drone is one button (spectacle, not a lesson); ADD TO SHIP is just the gate that makes reading the explanation non-skippable. Terminology (Rizel, 2026-06-10): in all player-facing copy the workers are called **subagents**, never drones — only the location keeps the name "The Drone Bay" (possible later rename; candidates discussed: The Fleet, Mission Control, fiction-patch "drones piloted by subagents"). Why: the word should make the real-world mapping unmistakable — these are agents working in parallel (or sequentially, player's choice) and you get insight into their work. Not metaphor decoration. See [[level2-archive-search-design]] and [[level2-design-direction]].

**REWORKED 2026-06-16 — starting-grid / mission-control rebuild.** Rizel disliked walking the island to scattered sites; redesigned the dispatch layer (NOT the explain/dispatch teaching, which is unchanged) into a fixed-camera racing-grid. droneBayView.js rewritten: dropped first-person (firstPerson.js), overhead map (overhead.js), pointer-lock, walk-up proximity, and the E-assign key. Now: fixed camera over 5 parallel lanes; subagents are **cars** (not drones) parked on a grid, rails drawn along terrain. **Click a car = arm; Space = green flag (launches all armed at once); click a moving car = nitro boost.** This one mechanic gives both sequential (arm-one-then-Space) and parallel (arm all five) — Rizel explicitly wanted both. Nitro = the engagement-during-travel + catch-up tool (3 charges/run, shown as dots in #db-systems-rows); Rizel didn't care what "powerups" meant, "just something where they go faster." Lanes are LOCAL to L2 (props placed at lane-ends, START_Z=64→FINISH_Z=-34, slight fan) and decoupled from SYSTEMS.pos — left SYSTEMS.pos untouched because launchView.js (L3) still reads it for its own island. Tunables: BASE_SPEED 1/14, BOOST_MULT 2.6, BOOST_DUR 2.2, NITRO_MAX 3, TOTAL_TIME 150. Kept: clock+panic sky, checkpoint cards, click-sealed-car→pre-filled explain→ADD TO SHIP→dispatch finish, win/fail. Open/untested live: car speed feel, whether any lane-end finish lands in water. Briefing + CONTROLS in index.html (#db-briefing) rewritten to match (click/Space, no WASD/E/M).

**TENSION REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch grid (drag + generic hazards).** Playtest of the starting-grid version: "kinda good but no tension." Diagnosed: cars were already on rails (just flipping a switch), clock far too generous (150s vs ~14s parallel), nitro had no job, parallel overlap means the slowest lane sets finish time so an aggregate clock CAN'T create parallel tension (mathematically — boosting only the slowest barely helps). Conclusion: parallel "chaos" tension MUST come from a per-lane failure stake, not the clock. Agreed direction with Rizel: **true drag + generic hazards** (rejected specialty-matching cars/Decoder/Repair — it's the "drone management" she already killed, blows the 15s onboarding, and telegraphs outcomes which guts the explain payoff). Built in droneBayView.js: cars now wait in a BAY (BAY_Z=START_Z+26=90), tracks start EMPTY. Verbs: **drag** a car bay→track (loads at line), **Space** green-flags all loaded, **click** a bogged running car to nitro through. Decoupled car pool (`cars[]`, state bay→loaded→running→done) from `lanes[]` (state empty→loaded→running→sealed→added); lane.carObj links them. Drag = mousedown/mousemove(>5px)/mouseup, ground-plane raycast for position, nearestEmptyLane (|Δx|<9 & z<BAY_Z-10) for drop; click vs drag disambiguated by movement. Each lane has a corruption patch (HAZARDS[]: 2 severe + 3 mild, staggered): inside, speed×HAZARD_SLOW(0.30); SEVERE lanes also fill `corrupt` over HAZARD_FILL(3.6s) → STALL (crawl at STALL_CRAWL 0.06, basically stuck) unless boosted; boost (nitro--) clears the patch + un-stalls. NITRO_MAX=3 < 5 cars = the triage choice (cover the 2 severe). **Clock is now the RESCUE RUN**: starts on first green flag (clockStarted), stops the moment allHome() (every lane sealed/added) — so reviewing explanations is calm/untimed (protects the lesson). TOTAL_TIME=50, BASE_SPEED=1/12, LOW_TIME=18/CRIT=8/PANIC=22 — all tunable, NOT yet playtested. Fail modes that create tension: sequential bleeds the clock, a car left in the bay never homes (clock kills you), wasting nitro on mild lanes → severe stalls → fail. Hazard discs (buildHazard) sit on tracks so you can read severity before launch; drop-target lane rails brighten cyan while dragging; is-stalled CSS row = red blink "BOGGED". Briefing/controls rewritten again (drag/Space/click). Rizel will feel-out & tune the constants (she did this for L1 timer).

**SCALE REBUILD 2026-06-16 — dispatch yard (12 jobs / 6 cars / reuse + free camera).** After the drag+hazards version, Rizel wanted (a) better visibility ("can't see beams/hazards, want to feel on the island") and (b) more multitasking via more tracks + fewer cars + reuse. Key design calls she made in discussion: **Level 3 stays FROZEN** (the 5 hero SYSTEMS untouched, still exported for L3); **explain+ADD TO SHIP stays REQUIRED on all 12** — she explicitly said the review IS the friction/lesson and chose "all twelve required, reading-fatigue be damned" (I'd argued dosage matters / required×12 = rubber-stamp; she overrode, fine — feel it in play). Built: `JOBS = [...SYSTEMS(5 hero), ...GENERIC_JOBS(7)]` = 12 tracks; generic jobs are L2-only (own ckpts/cards, reuse the 5 broken+5 upgrade prop builders, never seen by L3). 6 cars (N_CARS) < 12 jobs → **reuse loop**: car seals its job → detaches (lane.carObj=null, lane stays "sealed" for review) → car.state="returning" drives itself back to its home bay slot (RETURN_MULT 2.4) → "bay" → re-draggable. Review now triggers by **clicking the sealed JOB** (invisible reviewHit box at the anchor, jobAtPointer raycast) since the car has driven off — not by clicking the car. **Free mission-control camera**: orbit (drag empty background) + zoom (scroll wheel); spherical around camTarget(0,0,14), camRadius 56-210, camPhi 0.5-1.45, camTheta ±0.9. Pointer routing in onPointerDown: car hit → drag/boost; sealed-job hit → review; else → camera-orbit. **Volumetric hazards** (buildHazard: emissive shard cluster + additive dome + ground ring) replace flat decals so they read at the low orbit angle; **taller/brighter beams** (r1.0 h80). Side panel can't show 12 rows → **aggregate tally** (db-jobs/db-tally CSS): "online x/12", en route / to review / bogged counts, cars free n/6. Tunables (UNPLAYTESTED): TOTAL_TIME 80, N_CARS 6, NITRO_MAX 6, BASE_SPEED 1/12, RETURN_MULT 2.4, ~4 severe hazards (i%3). Lane layout 12-wide. **WATER/CAMERA FIX 2026-06-16** (Rizel: too bird's-eye, cars in the water, wants a spot on the island): terrain seaLevel=0 and land is only reliable within ~radius 60 of centre, so the original wide spread (finishX ±77) put cars in the sea. Compacted onto a centred land patch. Iterated again (Rizel: "big island mostly what I see, no water, beams not in water, a DOCK with the cars, view starts on the tracks"): current layout START_Z=34, FINISH_Z=-24, BAY_Z=48, startX (i-5.5)*6 finishX *7, baySlot *7 — verified with node script /tmp/landcheck.mjs sampling terrain.heightAt: min finish/beam height 4.24 (solid gold land, not shore), min track 1.02, bay 3.99. Built a real DOCK, then moved it OFF TO THE SIDE (Rizel: dock to the side, view should start on the tracks). Dock is now a pier on the +x edge: DOCK_X=46, cars in a z-column (DOCK_Z0=24, DOCK_DZ=-6, 6 slots z 24..-6), baySlot(i)={x:46,z:24-6i}, deck box (narrow x=10, long z) on 4 stilts + cyan rim, cars parked ON deck (home.y=deckY+0.35) facing -x (BAY_FACING=π/2, nose→-x toward the field). nearestEmptyLane now distance-to-start-point (hypot<9), geometry-agnostic so side-dock drops work. Then (Rizel: tracks should START near the beginning/front edge of the island + more zoom): pushed the whole field FORWARD toward the +z shore and NARROWED the fan so the forward outer lanes stay on land — START_Z=50, FINISH_Z=-12, LANE startX*4 finishX*5.5 (the island's high ground is central; the front is low shore, so a wide forward fan hits water — narrowing was the fix). Dock moved up alongside: DOCK_X=40, DOCK_Z0=44. Verified via /tmp/landcheck.mjs sweeping candidates: chosen layout "H" has min heights beam 3.3 / start 3.7 / track 3.3 / dock 2.2 (all on land). Camera zoomed in + front-biased: camTarget(0,1.5,24), camRadius 52 (zoom 36-150), camPhi 1.2, theta 0 — opens at the start of the tracks looking inward, dock at right. clamps theta ±0.8 / phi 0.72-1.36. NOTE the 12-lane WIDTH is the recurring constraint on both forward placement (outer lanes → water) and zoom (outer lanes → off-screen); narrowing the fan bought room but fewer lanes would buy more. KEY TRICK for "no water": tightened fog to 80-190 so the distant sea hazes into the sky colour instead of trying to win the geometry fight (you can't both hide water AND avoid bird's-eye on a round island — fog resolves it). Tracks are now SHORT (z 34→-24 ≈ 58u) so traversal is quick — clock/ speed likely need a retune. Onboarding copy STILL pending (2-beat A/B/C). NOTE: onboarding copy still PENDING — Rizel wants 2 beats (problem→solution, no em-dashes, every beat load-bearing); current file still has the 3-beat version; she was mid-deciding between options A/B/C when we pivoted to this rebuild.

**COMMAND-PASS / ORDER-TICKET-RUSH REBUILD 2026-06-16 (Overcooked).** Playtest verdict on the dispatch-yard: "it's the view, it's not fun." Rizel's real want: the frantic "omg how can I finish 12 in this time AND run entire explain," and "it doesn't remind me of any similar games" — i.e. L2 needs a recognizable game genre like L1 does. Diagnosis: (a) cars-on-tracks isn't a known fun loop; (b) the dispatch logistics and the explain LESSON were bolted together but didn't reinforce — explain was the calm tax AFTER the fun (old build deliberately STOPPED the clock for review to "protect the lesson"). Fix = flip it: **the timed game IS clearing the explain queue.** Job count stays 12 (she's fine with it — overrode my "fewer jobs" pitch again; it's the view, not the count). Picked **Overcooked order-ticket rush** (she chose it from 3 ASCII-preview skins: ticket-rush vs conveyor-inspector vs control-wall-whackamole). REJECTED per-ticket expiry — she caught that it lies about the fiction ("it's not real food and the record always exists"; a checkpoint is permanent, explain works forever). So tension = the **launch window clock that drains FASTER the bigger the unreviewed pile** (ship can't stabilise on unaccounted work); nothing is ever lost. Built: dropped cars/lanes/drag/nitro/hazards/dock/free-camera/terrain entirely. New **fixed command-pass view**: camera (0,11.5,27)→(0,3.2,-1); 6 subagent stations (buildWorker, orbs brighten as their job nears done) in a row at z=-8; a scrolling chevron belt (makeBeltTexture) at z=2; a SHIP-SYSTEMS hull board (buildShipBoard, 12 pips 2×6) at back that lights a pip green per added system. Each finished job rides up as a **slate** (buildSlate: slab+cyan rim + the improvised UPGRADE model on top under an ice block + makeIdSprite ckpt + makeLabelSprite name) — the bow→sword reveal is preserved (you see a weird star-dome arrive and wonder, explain answers). Loop: Space×2 briefing (now 2 load-bearing beats, no em-dashes) → **DISPATCH STEP** (added 2026-06-16 at Rizel's ask — the command-pass build had dropped the delegate half; she wanted "send out the subagents as a step" back, like old E-assign/drag): the 6 stations sit idle showing a pulsing ground ring + "SEND ▸" label; **DISPATCH→CONVEYOR→EXPLAIN→DRAG-TO-MATCH 2026-06-17** (current; supersedes the simple-station build below). After the strip-down, Rizel still wanted the matching/drag step AND affirmed "the conveyor belt was good" and "I dispatch them first." Synthesis she converged on (clarity was the real ask all along; the OLD routing felt bad only because it used cryptic bay codes + explain spoon-fed the answer — the dragging was never the problem): each part has a LABELED slot on the ship hull (icon+name, e.g. 🔧 Engine). Loop: slot breaks (red, "▶ CLICK TO DISPATCH") → **click slot = dispatch a subagent** (drone flies up, welds) → its finished checkpoint **rides up the CONVEYOR belt** as a slate labeled with the same icon+name → **click slate = `entire checkpoint explain`** (card shows what it did; also auto-explains if you just grab it) → **DRAG the slate up onto its matching slot** (same name+icon; wrong slot bounces, no penalty) → snaps in green. All 6 → `entire dispatch`. STAKE = launch clock (TOTAL_TIME 90). CLARITY = slate & slot share name+icon + you watch it travel belt→its labeled home (fixes "things go to random places"). Juggle from BREAK_TIMES waves [0,1.5,4,9,14,20] + staggered returns (PART_FIX_TIME 4 ±1.8). 6 relatable PART_DATA (Engine/Air/Battery/Radio/Steering/Lights, whimsical becomes). Reuses buildSlate (belt slate), makeBeltTexture, buildDrone, prop builders. Drag: mousedown picks belt slate, >6px move = dragging (slate floats at DRAG_DEPTH 22 under cursor), drop raycasts slot hitboxes, install if slot.idx===slate.idx. SYSTEMS still frozen/exported for L3 (L3 keeps old jargon names — align later). VERIFIED headless (CDP real mouse + synthetic drag): break→dispatch→fix→belt→explain→drag→install→1/6 online, zero exceptions. UNCOMMITTED. Tunables: TOTAL_TIME, BREAK_TIMES, PART_FIX_TIME, DRAG_DEPTH. Dead code from older builds still in file (buildShipBoard, db-board markup/css, board constants) — clean up later.

**NO MATCH HIGHLIGHT + GRADE AT THE END 2026-06-17** (Rizel: "I don't like that it lights up when I'm trying to match...too obvious...I should just drop it in the spots, and then either at the end or after it's placed it gives me an indicator the match was correct"). Removed the drag-time green "DROP HERE" target glow entirely (the spoiler). New flow: drag an explained block, drop it into ANY empty square (no hint) and it STAYS there (state "placed", `placedIn` = that lane; reuses the holder); a placed-but-ungraded bay glows amber. Matching is only CHECKED at the end: when all 6 are placed the terminal prompts `entire dispatch`, which `gradeAndDispatch()` runs — correct ones lock in GREEN (✓ spark, state online), wrong ones flash red and ride BACK OUT on the belt (re-added to belted, state→review) with "✗ N in the wrong bay — re-match and dispatch again"; win only when all correct. Chose end-grading over per-placement (fits her no-spoilers push; she listed it first). Key impl: installPart→placePart(p, sl) seats into the TARGET square `sl.slot.userData.holder` (NOT p's own); squareOccupant(slIdx) blocks double-drops; slot render shows amber(placed)/green(online), no isTarget highlight; clock keeps running until graded-correct (mild pressure to fix). **BUG FOUND+FIXED in test:** first pass wrote `sl.userData.holder` (sl is a PART, has no userData) → TypeError; correct is `sl.slot.userData.holder`. VERIFIED headless via CDP with a real projected drag (had to replicate the camera projection AND fetch the canvas rect — canvas is 1280×713 not full-window, inset by the HUD; projecting into the rect was the fix): dispatched all 6 → explained → dropped all 6 into arbitrary bays (all placed, off belt, NO highlight) → `entire dispatch` → graded, all 6 flagged wrong, ejected back to belt with the ✗ message, zero exceptions. Win path (all-correct → online + sendDispatch) is trivial/shared, not auto-verified (can't match identities headlessly). To flip to per-placement feedback: have placePart grade immediately instead of gradeAndDispatch. UNCOMMITTED. Test harness: /tmp/gradetest.mjs (projector + canvas-rect trick — reuse for future drag tests).

**DOTS ALL PRESENT + DECOUPLED FROM BLOCKS 2026-06-17** (Rizel: "I want the red dots to already be there and not show which block they're associated with lol"). Two changes: (1) all 6 little red dots are lit on the board FROM THE START (no timed breaking) — removed BREAK_TIMES, breakAt, the timed breakPart loop, and breakPart() itself; parts now init state "broken". (2) Dots DECOUPLED from parts: dots are now generic job markers (`jobDots[] = {group,pos,taken}`), not owned by a part. Clicking a dot calls `dispatchFromDot(jd)` which pulls the NEXT part from a shuffled `pendingQueue` — so the dot's position never reveals which sealed block you'll get (you only learn via explain). The subagent flies to the clicked dot (`d.weldAt = jd.pos`; weldPose(at) takes a Vector3 now), consumes it (taken=true, group hidden → dim socket shows). dotAtPointer returns a jobDot; onPointerUp dispatches it. Per-frame pulse iterates jobDots. resetLevel re-rolls pendingQueue + slotLanes, un-takes all dots, parts back to "broken". Pacing note: timed break-waves are GONE; the juggle now comes from staggered per-part fix-time RETURNS (2.5–7.5s) + the clock + sealed-backlog drain. Dispatch is a quick burst (6 dots, 6 subagents, 1:1) then it's all about keeping up with returns/explain/match. Verified headless: start shows "subagents 6/6 red 6" (all dots lit), clicking dispatches (red→4, subagents→4), zero exceptions; screenshot confirms full board of little red dots at start. UNCOMMITTED. Supersedes the per-part-dot version below.

**DISPATCH BOARD WITH LITTLE DOTS 2026-06-17** (Rizel clarified: "little red dots ON A BOARD — I click the dots, the subagents go over to those red dots, then I put the items in the squares"). Corrects the rail version below: it's now a framed BOARD panel (dispatchBoard box + boardEdge frame + "⚠ DISPATCH" title sprite) on the lower hull (DOT_Y 4.6, z=SHIP_Z-0.5), with a row of dim sockets (CircleGeometry, one per lane) and a LITTLE red dot (buildDispatchDot now small: 0.34 sphere + flat circle halo, hit sphere 1.0) on each socket, lit only when that part is broken. Drone bay MOVED to the front (home z=6, y=0.5) so a dispatched subagent visibly flies UP and OVER to its dot then back (was flying from z=-24 behind the board, which clipped it). Weld pose `weldPose(p)=dotPos+(0,-2.0,2.6)` hovers the drone just in front of/level with the dot. Verified headless: clicking a little dot at its real screen coords dispatches (free 6→5, the dot goes dark) and a screenshot caught the drone mid-flight to the board; zero exceptions. NOTE on screenshot coords: captures are 1280×800 but the preview is ~half-scale — multiply preview px by ~2 to get click coords (wasted a cycle clicking the half-scale positions). Tunables: board size/position, dot size, drone bay z (they're a bit prominent in the foreground), weldPose offsets. Below = the superseded rail version:

**3D DISPATCH DOTS — DRONES FLY TO THEM 2026-06-17** (Rizel: "the subagents should fly to the red dots"). Problem: red dots lived in a 2D HTML panel, but drones flew to the ship SQUARES (the drag-destinations) — backwards now that dispatch & matching are split. Fix: dots are now real scene objects the drones fly to. New `buildDispatchDot()` (red emissive sphere + additive halo + invisible hit sphere). A `dispatchRail` bar + "⚠ DISPATCH" sprite sit on the LOWER hull (DOT_Y 4.6, z=SHIP_Z+0.6) BELOW the install squares (SHIP_Y 9.5) — clean vertical read: failures (mid) → belt (bottom) → install squares (top). Each part owns a `dot` at a FIXED lane (slotXForLane(i), anonymous so order≠identity), `dotPos`; hidden until broken. breakPart shows it + sparks at dotPos; dispatchPart hides it. Drone flight retargeted: out/back lerp to `dotPos.setY(DOT_Y+2)` (was slotPos) so the subagent physically flies to the red dot and welds there, then the sealed block rides the belt. New `dotAtPointer()` raycast; onPointerUp dispatches on dot click (slot-click dispatch already gone); onPointerMove cursor = pointer over slate OR dot. Per-frame: visible dots pulse (emissive/halo/scale). HTML #db-board now fully retired — renderBoard() just keeps it hidden; "subagents X/6" free count moved into the #db-systems tally (is-free); removed the boardRowsEl click handler. resetLevel hides all dots + re-rolls as before. Copy updated (briefing beat1, start tutorial, dispatch teach → "a subagent flies out to fix it"). VERIFIED headless (CDP screen-sweep clicks on the 3D dots): dispatch fires (free 6→4, red 3→1, sealed pile grows), board hidden, zero exceptions; screenshot shows the ⚠ DISPATCH rail with glowing red dots mid-screen, shuffled install squares on top, belt below. UNCOMMITTED. Tunables: DOT_Y, rail z, dot size/pulse.

**ANONYMOUS DISPATCH + SEALED REVEAL 2026-06-17** (Rizel's redesign, agreed in convo). Splits L2 into a "dumb half" and a "smart half" and finally makes `explain` load-bearing. (1) **Dispatch board = anonymous red dots only** — "doesn't need to say battery or anything." renderBoard now renders ONE identical red dot per BROKEN part (not all 6, no first-letter label, no name in aria/title), plus a dynamic note ("click a dot to send a subagent" / "all subagents busy" / "no failures"). index.html board simplified (title "⚠ DISPATCH", "subagents free X/6", dropped static sub). So dispatch is pure reflex: dot appears → click → subagent flies. (2) **Dispatch moved OFF the ship slots entirely** — removed slot-click dispatch (onPointerUp) and the red-broken slot rendering + "CLICK TO DISPATCH" hint. The hull squares are now PURE destinations: neutral/labeled at all times, they DON'T reveal what's broken or where a fix goes; only the square you're actively dragging an explained slate onto lights green ("DROP HERE"). Resolves the old two-dispatch-surface redundancy (board AND slots) in favor of board-only. (3) **Returned work is sealed/anonymous until explained** — the belt slate was already just an ice block (upgradeModel hidden until explain; no label existed), so the only change was the REVEAL: explainPart now attaches a `${icon} ${name}` tag sprite to the slate (and shows the upgrade model). So you genuinely can't tell what a subagent fixed until you run `entire checkpoint explain` — THEN you read it and find the matching (shuffled, labeled) ship square. This is the Diner-Dash "ticket is a mystery until you check it" beat and it makes explain non-skippable (fixes old open-Q#2). Loop now: anonymous red dot → click to send → sealed mystery block rides belt → click = explain reveals identity → drag to its matching labeled square → all 6 → `entire dispatch`. NOTE: with anonymous dots you can NOT pick the slow Engine to send first (don't know which dot is which) — variable fix times still stagger the belt, just no dispatch-time strategy; Rizel accepted this. Matching squares = the SHIP HULL (kept the repair fiction; didn't switch to a separate abstract grid — that option was offered, defaulted to hull). Copy updated (briefing beat 2, prompts, drag-bounce msg, CTA). CSS: added .db-board-note; .db-dot-label + is-working/onbelt/review/online dot rules now unused (harmless). Verified headless: anonymous dots (0 labels), neutral hull, sealed ice blocks on belt, free 6/6, zero exceptions. UNCOMMITTED.

**PER-PART FIX TIMES 2026-06-17** (Rizel: "some things should take longer to fix than others"). Was uniform PART_FIX_TIME 4.0 ±1.8 jitter → no reason to think about dispatch order. Now each PART_DATA entry has its own `fix` (seconds), roughly scaled to how big the repair sounds / its session attempts: Engine 7.5, Radio 6.5 (3 attempts), Battery 5.5, Steering 4.5, Air 3.5, Lights 2.5. Drone weld sets `fixT = (part.data.fix ?? PART_FIX_FALLBACK 4.0) + Math.random()*PART_FIX_JITTER(0.7)` — small jitter, identity dominates. Tied to part identity (NOT shuffled like layout/break-order) so it's learnable (Overcooked cook-times) and the belt fills unevenly: Lights returns in 2.5s while Engine's still welding at 7.5s, so WHICH you send first matters. Currently learn-by-play (no pre-dispatch duration tell; "working…" slot state + the 3 in-flight are the visible signal). Verified headless, zero exceptions. UNCOMMITTED.

**SHUFFLED MATCHING 2026-06-17** (Rizel: "one of the boring parts is that the matching will always be in order"). Real flaw: parts broke in a fixed index order [0,1.5,4,9,14,20] → dispatched in order → returned in order → and slots were laid out left-to-right in that SAME order, so the leftmost belt slate always belonged to the leftmost slot = straight-up drag, never read a label = a conveyor, not matching. Fix: `shuffled(n)` Fisher-Yates helper; per run re-roll **slotLanes** (which horizontal lane each part's slot occupies) AND **breakSlots** (which BREAK_TIME each part gets), both re-rolled in resetLevel too. Slot x now `slotXForLane(slotLanes[i])`, breakAt `BREAK_TIMES[breakSlots[i]]`. Now the belt slate never lines up under its slot — you read the icon+name and route it (true Diner-Dash lookup). Verified headless: slot labels render in shuffled order (e.g. Battery·Steering·Lights·Air·Radio·Engine), loop runs, zero exceptions. Nothing assumed index-order layout (slotAtPointer reads userData.slotIdx, belt layout independent, drone fly-out uses p.slotPos). UNCOMMITTED.

**PRESSURE SIMPLIFY + DEAD-CODE CLEANUP 2026-06-17** (on the DISPATCH→CONVEYOR→EXPLAIN→DRAG-TO-MATCH build above; my "take a swing" after Rizel asked what I thought of L2's state). Two changes, no design/loop change: (1) **collapsed three coupled clock-drains into ONE visible pressure.** Old drain was `dt·(1 + RAIL_DRAIN·beltCount + URGENCY_DRAIN·urgencyPressure)` PLUS a separate per-broken-part urgency meter (URGENCY_RATE) PLUS board urgency rings — the player felt the clock lurch with no visible cause (exactly the "overcomplicating" smell). Removed the urgency system ENTIRELY (constants URGENCY_RATE/URGENCY_DRAIN/TRIAGE_PANIC, per-part `.urgency` field, `urgencyPressure()`, board is-urgent/is-warn classes + `.db-dot-ring` conic-gradient CSS + db-pip-urgent keyframes, the "URGENT" slot hint). Now the clock ticks at 1× and the ONLY accelerator is **sealed/unreviewed blocks on the belt**: `drain = dt·(1 + SEAL_DRAIN·unreviewedCount())`, SEAL_DRAIN 0.18, BACKLOG_PANIC 4. Chose unreviewed (not all-belt) on purpose — running `explain` immediately relieves pressure, so explain is the pressure-relief verb (pedagogically honest; fiction: "ship can't stabilise on work nobody has accounted for"). panicFactor + the backlog prompt now key off unreviewedCount too. (2) **Deleted dead code**: GENERIC_JOBS (7 jobs), JOBS, buildShipBoard (the clickable board is HTML #db-board, not this 3D helper) — droneBayView.js ~1250→1070 lines. KEPT buildBrokenStrut/buildGravSkid + SYSTEMS + UPGRADE_BUILDERS (L3/launchView.js imports them). Verified: esbuild clean (both droneBayView + launchView), headless CDP smoke (real DOM clicks on board dots) ran the loop with 3 sealed blocks driving the drain + reddening the panic sky, ZERO exceptions/console errors. UNCOMMITTED. Still OPEN (raised to Rizel, her call): (a) two redundant dispatch surfaces — the HTML #db-board AND the 3D ship slots both dispatch; with urgency gone the board is just a mini-map, candidate to remove; (b) explain is gated before install but not *absorbed* (you can match off the slot label without reading the card); (c) L3 still uses old jargon names. Awaiting playtest of pace/feel before tuning TOTAL_TIME 90 / SEAL_DRAIN / BREAK_TIMES.

**SIMPLE STATION REBUILD 2026-06-17** (supersedes ALL the dispatch-board/conveyor/triage machinery below — Rizel: "we're overcomplicating the game... we want a challenge, something on the line, but not too many steps"; also "nav core/ignition coil names are unrelatable" and the conveyor/board lacked Diner-Dash clarity = "you don't know it's not going to random things"). Stripped to the bone: ONE screen, the ship as an arc of 6 part-STATIONS; the whole lifecycle of a part lives at its own station (clarity fix — no more board→generic-drone→anonymous-conveyor→pip scatter). Loop, 3 clicks same spot: part breaks (flashing red, "▶ CLICK TO FIX") → click → a subagent drone flies over & welds → part returns changed under ice ("✓ CLICK TO REVIEW") → click → `entire checkpoint explain` card → accept (Y / ADD TO SHIP) → green/online. All 6 online → type `entire dispatch` → win. STAKE = launch clock (TOTAL_TIME 90); fail at 0:00. Overcooked feel = PACING not mechanics: parts break in overlapping waves (BREAK_TIMES [0,1.5,4,9,14,20]) and subagents return staggered (PART_FIX_TIME 4.5 ±1.8), so you juggle "some need sending + some need reviewing" at once (verified: broken count hit 3 while 1 in review). RELATABLE names+icons, L2-only `PART_DATA` (6): 🔧 Engine, 🫁 Air, 🔋 Battery, 📡 Radio, 🧭 Steering, 💡 Lights (improvised results stay whimsical: Plasma Ring/Garden Pod/Cell Bloom/Signal Spire/Star Dome/Aurora Array — that surprise is why explain is fun). Reuses broken+upgrade prop builders, buildDrone (octa+halo), makeIceBlock/makeIdSprite, terminal for explain+dispatch, panic sky, win/fail. **SYSTEMS export left UNCHANGED & frozen so Level 3 is untouched** — but L3 still shows the OLD jargon names (nav core/antenna/struts); align L3 to the relatable names later for cross-level consistency. Dead code remains in droneBayView.js from prior versions (buildSlate, buildShipBoard, makeBeltTexture, dispatch-board constants, SYMPTOM_BY_JOB, GENERIC_JOBS/JOBS) — harmless, clean up later. #db-board markup/CSS now unused (hidden). UNCOMMITTED; tunables to feel: TOTAL_TIME, BREAK_TIMES, PART_FIX_TIME, PART_FLY. Verified headless (CDP, real mouse clicks): break→send→fix→review→explain→accept→online, zero exceptions. Older over-built versions kept below for history:

**DISPATCH BOARD / TRIAGE 2026-06-16** (supersedes click-the-drone dispatch): Rizel tried adding her idea-list "#4 Dispatch Board" but coded it as a routing-match on the REVIEW side (explain printed the install bay, you clicked the matching bay; wrong=−4s) and said it "wasn't hitting fun/challenge." Diagnosis I gave her: matching a printed label to a labeled slot is a clerical chore (no decision), the penalty punishes misclicks, and it turned `explain` into keyword-hunting. Principle: dispatcher fun = TRIAGE (more demand than you can serve, decide what to tackle next), not matching. She chose "Assign + triage (real #4)". BUILT (working, verified headless, zero exceptions; UNPLAYTESTED feel): reset to the committed clean view, scrapped routing-match, restored simple explain→Y/ADD. New dispatch half = a DOM **#db-board** (left panel) listing OPEN failing systems (name + symptom from SYMPTOM_BY_JOB + a rising urgency bar). **Click a board row → a free drone flies out** to that job (drones are now a pure reusable POOL of 6, no pre-assigned queues; phase idle=free→out→work→back→idle). Jobs trickle on (INITIAL_OPEN 4, SPAWN_EVERY 3.2s, capped MAX_OPEN 6 concurrent). Urgency rises (URGENCY_RATE ~1/13s) once clock starts; the launch clock drains by dt·(1+RAIL_DRAIN·passCount+URGENCY_DRAIN·boardTotalUrgency) so ignored urgent jobs bleed time = the triage stake. Board rows go amber>0.33 / red-pulse>0.66. Review half unchanged. LAYOUT BUG fixed: tall board overlapped the front pass slot (slate unclickable behind it) → capped MAX_OPEN 6 + CSS max-height 300px so board bottom (~295px) stays above the belt (~y470). index.html added #db-board markup; style.css added .db-board/.db-job-row/.db-job-fill styles + .is-failing tally. Old per-drone click-dispatch / Space-dispatch / SEND cue all removed. NOT committed yet. Earlier dispatch model (kept for history below):
**click a subagent to dispatch it ONE AT A TIME** (Space = send the NEXT single idle drone, not all — Rizel 2026-06-16: "why did all the subagents leave at the same time, I wanted to dispatch them one at a time"; the earlier Space=dispatch-all was my add and is removed/repurposed to next-single), and the launch clock does NOT start until the first dispatch (clockStarted on dispatchWorker). She chose tactile per-subagent sending for the delegate feel + a parallel-vs-sequential stagger choice. **RE-SEND 2026-06-16** (Rizel: "we only sent out 6 subagents, so how did we get all 6/12 fixed records" — the silent auto-2nd-job broke the count's logic): each drone now does ONE job per dispatch, flies home and goes idle (SEND cue returns), and YOU re-dispatch it for its 2nd job. 6 drones × you sending each twice = 12 records — the count is a direct result of player action, and "send subagents" stays a live verb the whole run (you re-send while reviewing; an idle drone with a job left = work not happening = the clock keeps bleeding). sendable(w)=phase==="idle"&&queue.length; "done"=no jobs left, parked dim. **DRONE LOOK + VISIBLE FLY-OUT 2026-06-16** (Rizel: subagents "should look like something — drones/robots," and she wants to SEE them "fly off and work on repairing the things before it piles up a backlog"): buildWorker→**buildDrone**. (First pass was a quadcopter w/ rotors+eye+welder; Rizel preferred an EARLIER drone look from a past version. **Found via the entire-search agent in commit 0553cd7** "Add Level 2 'The Drone Bay'" — a dark metallic OctahedronGeometry(0.9) body, emissive 0x2a8aa6, wrapped in a spinning cyan TorusGeometry halo (orig 1.0r, now 1.25r); halo spin speeds up idle→flight→work like the original. RESTORED that.) It hovers on a landing pad with the SEND ▸ ring/label + a hover underglow; welds with amber sparks under the body at the hull. On dispatch the drone runs a phase machine (idle→out→work→back→done): lifts off and FLIES to a hull work-site (up at SITE_Y 8, back toward the ship board at SITE_Z -16) over FLY_DUR 1.1s, WELDS in place (eye brightens, amber sparks at the tool tip) for WORK_TIME, emits the finished slate to the pass, then flies home (re-send for its 2nd job — see RE-SEND above; parks dim once its queue is empty). So you watch the crew work the hull before any backlog builds. Verified headlessly (CDP): fly-out, weld-sparks, flood, explain, add — zero exceptions. Round-robin 2 jobs each (WORK_TIME 6.5 ±2.5 jitter) → finished job pushed to `pass[]`, slate slides in from ENTRANCE_X to its slot (VISIBLE_SLOTS=5, SLOT_X0 -16 / SLOT_DX 8; extras back up off-screen but still count). **Click slate = run explain** (focuses + flips, card in repurposed #terminal as a NON-blocking panel — pointer stays live so the pass keeps flooding while you read); **Y or ADD TO SHIP button** = added → slate flies to its hull pip, ice melts, pip greens. All 12 added → clock stops, terminal switches to blocking "report" mode → type `entire dispatch` → win. Fail = clock hits 0 (drain = dt·(1 + RAIL_DRAIN·pass.length), RAIL_DRAIN 0.14). Tunables (UNPLAYTESTED, Rizel will feel out like the L1 timer): TOTAL_TIME 75, WORK_TIME 6.5, WORK_JITTER 2.5, RAIL_DRAIN 0.14, VISIBLE_SLOTS 5, BACKLOG_PANIC 5, N_WORKERS 6. L3 untouched — SYSTEMS (with .pos) + UPGRADE_BUILDERS still exported and consumed by launchView.js. index.html: db-fail copy updated (no more "use the fleet"); db-briefing already the 2-beat mission-banner; all db-/term-/cta- CSS classes reused. **VERIFIED via headless-Chrome CDP drive (node /tmp/cdp.mjs): full loop runs with ZERO exceptions — briefing→flood→click-explain→Y-add→pip green→pass reflow.** Screenshots confirm layout reads well; slates are large/slightly cramped at SLOT_DX 8 (candidate tune). Dev server: `npm run dev` (was on :5174). OPEN/likely-to-tune: clock vs work-speed feel, slate spacing/scale, whether the non-blocking panel wants a visible "focused slate" tie-line.

**PATIENCE / SPOIL REBUILD — Diner Dash customers 2026-06-17 (CURRENT, supersedes the abstract-drain + reveal-on-explain model).** Rizel's review-driven redesign of the match step. Chain of decisions: (1) she disliked that `explain` slapped a `🔧 Engine` tag on the block that exactly matched the bay's `🔧 Engine` label → trivial icon-match, zero deduction. (2) She wants the block to STAY SEALED — explain only opens the report card; you READ what the subagent did and DEDUCE which bay it belongs to. No identity ever shown on the block (rejected even putting the neutral ckpt hash on it: "they will get it from clicking explain"). To tell sealed blocks apart / re-read, you re-click → explain card reopens. (3) The ICE is the patience meter (her Diner-Dash/Overcooked framing — "what happens when a customer waits too long"). It ages icy-blue→amber→red and visibly MELTS (shrinks/sinks/flickers). (4) If it fully melts the work is LOST: block leaves the belt, its red dot RETURNS to the dispatch board, that part goes back on pendingQueue → you must re-dispatch a subagent (cost = the time, against the launch clock). (5) She rejected my "maxes out the drain" option as unreadable jargon — the whole point was to KILL the invisible background drain and make pressure concrete/physical. IMPL in droneBayView.js: removed SEAL_DRAIN + the short-lived STALE_* stale-reviewed-block drain + BACKLOG_PANIC; clock now drains only `dt·(1 + DOT_DRAIN·activeDotHeat())` (dot heat kept = the dispatch-side pressure). Added PATIENCE=18s (set in partToBelt, counts DOWN every frame for every belted block, paused for the block in-hand while dragging), `urgencyOf(p)=1−patience/PATIENCE`, `meltingCount()`, `spoilPart(p)` (splices belted, removes slateMesh, p→broken, re-lights a taken jobDot, pushes idx to pendingQueue). ICE colour/melt in the belt loop keys off urgencyOf with ICE_WARM_AT 0.5 / ICE_MELT_AT 0.78 and colours ICE_FRESH/ICE_WARM_C/ICE_HOT_C. explainPart no longer reveals model/tag/ice — reveal of the upgrade model + ice-off now happens at INSTALL (placePart) as the payoff. Wrong-bay drop bounces WITHOUT naming the part (no spoiler). panicFactor now reddens sky from meltingCount + dot heat. Briefing beat 2 + prompts + header comment rewritten to the patience/melt fiction. Verified: ES-module import clean (NOT headless-loop-verified this session — needs a playtest). Tunables UNPLAYTESTED: PATIENCE 18 (likely tight since deduction needs reading time), ICE_WARM_AT, ICE_MELT_AT. NOTE: deduction is still only as hard as the explain card copy, which currently names the system ("engine was seized…") — if Rizel wants real deduction, obscure the system name in PART_DATA.did/became and lean on `symptom`. Builds on the drop-time grading below.

**GRADE AT DROP (not at dispatch) 2026-06-17** (precedes the patience rebuild above, same session). Rizel: "if u put it in the wrong square it bounces back and tells u that's the wrong square." Moved grading from `entire dispatch` to the moment of drop: placePart now checks `sl.idx !== p.idx` and, if wrong, bounces the block to the belt with a message + red spark (only the correct bay sticks). gradeAndDispatch simplified to a pure lock-in (wrong placements can no longer reach it). So `entire dispatch` is now just the "file the day" command, never a reveal.

Done — Level 2 now works the way you described. Here's the new loop:

**The block stays frozen.** `explain` no longer slaps a label on it — it just opens the report card. You read what the subagent *did* and **deduce** which bay it belongs to. The block never tells you. (If you lose track of which is which, click it again to re-read.)

**The ice is the patience meter** — exactly like a Diner Dash / Overcooked customer:
- Fresh = icy blue → it ages to **amber** → **red** → then visibly **melts** (shrinks, sinks, flickers).
- If it fully melts, **the work is lost**: the block leaves the belt, its **red dot comes back on the dispatch board**, and you have to dispatch a subagent to redo it. The punishment is the time, against the launch clock.

**The reveal is the payoff at install** — when you drag a block into its *correct* bay, the ice comes off and the improvised part (plasma ring, star dome…) appears. Wrong bay just bounces it back, *without* telling you what it was (no spoiler).

I also **ripped out the invisible drain** entirely — that "maxes out the drain" thing you (rightly) couldn't parse. The launch clock just ticks down now; all the belt pressure is the concrete, visible melting ice.
